ground_truth.title: Everything You Need to Know About Online Tutoring
ground_truth.author: Sasha Blakeley
ground_truth.main_content:
Recent years have seen a boom in the need for and prevalence of online learning systems for students of all ages. With this major change in education, many students have been turning to online tutoring to get extra support with their schoolwork. Working with a tutor can be helpful for a variety of reasons, and tutoring, whether online or in person, is something that many students may want to consider at some point in their academic careers. 

Although tutoring is a common and effective choice for many students, finding and choosing the right tutor can be overwhelming, and students and parents often have questions. Some tutoring services also have different educational approaches that suit some students better than others. This guide will give you all the information you need when it comes to deciding if online tutoring is right for you or your child. You will also get important insight into finding the right tutor for your needs and maintaining productive communication with your tutor. 

What Is Online Tutoring?

Some tutoring services involve the student and tutor meeting face to face, but others offer their tutoring online. An online tutor, sometimes called a virtual tutor, works with a student over an online video chat. This can be efficient for both student and tutor, cutting down on travel time and providing an accessible and COVID-safe option for educational help. 

Tutors fill a specific role in the educational process. They are typically not teachers and do not have to have teacher certification. They may have past teaching experience as well as subject matter expertise. Essentially, a tutor's main role is one of support. 

They work with a student's school material to fill in gaps, review material, and help explain and practice concepts that students might be struggling with. A tutor may, for example work with a student on their science curriculum, explaining the material, creating practice activities, and answering questions so that when the student returns to school, they feel prepared. 

While tutors do not generally provide the kind of detailed initial instruction that teachers do in schools, they do get to work one-on-one with students to understand their particular needs and areas of interest. Tutoring is by no means equivalent to or a replacement for in-class education, but it can complement that education and give students a more complete academic understanding. 

How Does Online Tutoring Work?

Online tutoring can work in a variety of ways depending on a number of factors. The tutoring service you choose, the age of the student in question, and the student's needs will all come together to inform the structure of your online tutoring experience. Generally speaking, online tutoring will function like an extra-curricular activity; it will take place a given number of times per week, often once or twice but more if needed, usually for an hour or two each session. Tutoring takes place outside of regular school hours and is usually on a set schedule for several weeks. 

Online tutoring will vary based on the approach of the tutoring organization or tutor in question. The student's needs will also make a difference: if an individual needs general help on a daily basis for an hour after school, that is well within the scope of normal online tutoring. If, on the other hand, a student only needs help with their biology course, sessions once or twice a week might be more appropriate. The great thing about tutoring is that it is far more personalized than regular educational systems, so there is really no limit to the permutations that are possible when organizing the right tutoring setup for a specific student. 

Many people think of tutoring as a service that helps struggling students with a specific subject so that they can stay at the same level as their peers. This is certainly one application of tutoring, called subject-based tutoring. However, tutors can also help students in other contexts. They can provide holistic tutoring that teaches broadly applicable academic skills, or they can support students as they complete their homework. Students who are studying for a major test or who are taking challenging courses for academic enrichment can also benefit from tutors' expertise to give them a boost. 

1. Subject-Based Tutoring 
Subject-based tutoring is what most people think of when they think of tutoring. Students who need math help because they are struggling to keep up with the material might have a math tutor who will explain concepts to them in more detail. A student who finds their schoolteacher's explanation of cellular respiration baffling could benefit from working with a science tutor. 
Many online tutors have a detailed knowledge of or interest in a specific set of subjects for a given age range of students. This kind of expertise can be helpful because it means that the tutor will likely know the subject very well and will know how to explain the relevant concepts effectively. They might use students' school material to explore ideas in a novel way that makes more sense to their pupils. They may also play educational games or even create practice quizzes to evaluate students' understanding and progress. Subject-based tutoring can be great for improving students' confidence and helping them feel more ready to tackle the material they are learning in class. 

2. Holistic Tutoring and Help with Homework 
One of the benefits of online tutoring services is that they can be as specialized or as broad as needed. Some students may not have trouble with individual subjects so much as they struggle with generally applicable academic skills. For instance, taking notes, keeping organized, and studying for tests are skills that all students must learn, but they might find they are never explicitly taught. Holistic tutoring can help students become better prepared for all their academic work. Some tutors could also work on skills like reading and writing fluency that are not necessarily connected to an individual subject but that are indispensable for academic success. 
Another possible application of tutoring is giving students structured help with homework. Some tutors use established teaching methods to help their pupils improve their focus and manage their time. For some, just having someone there to supervise them and to answer questions while they work through their homework can be immensely valuable. Balancing many subjects and completing work in a timely manner, just like other academic skills, is an important skillset that is not often taught explicitly to students, which can leave some individuals floundering without the help of an expert who can impart valuable tips and tricks. 

3. Test Preparation and Enrichment 
There is a common misconception that tutoring is exclusively for students who are having trouble keeping up in school and that students who excel have no need for extra support. In fact, this is untrue for many people. Students who are advanced in one or more academic subjects sometimes find tutoring helpful as a form of enrichment, allowing them to get ahead and practice concepts that are more complex than they are learning in school. 
Some students may deliberately enroll in more challenging courses to help feed their academic interests and tutors can give them the help that they need to succeed in those courses and to get as much out of their experiences as possible. For instance, a high school student might excel at math and want to skip a grade of math and go straight into an advanced calculus course. A tutor with math expertise can help them accelerate through any material they may be missing by advancing to a higher-level course, helping them cover a year of math instruction in a few months. 
Tutoring is not just for K-12 students. Online college tutoring can be a great option for students who need to study for many college-level exams. Exam prep with the help of a tutor can help reduce academic stress and ultimately allow them to succeed. Anyone who is planning on taking an important test might feel that a few hours a week of structured, assisted study time could make all the difference in the exam's outcome. Some tutors specialize in SAT or ACT® test preparation or other college intake examinations. Ultimately, tutoring is a highly flexible educational tool that can be applied to almost any academic need. 

What Do I Need for Online Tutoring?

Some parents and students may be wondering what kind of equipment they need to make the most of online tutoring platforms. Making sure that you have the correct setup for live tutoring sessions is very important when it comes to making the most of online tutoring. By far the most important element of any online tutoring setup is a good internet connection. Online tutoring takes place primarily over video chat, so it is essential that those on both ends of the connection can see and hear each other clearly. For families who have already made the transition to online learning in the past, this setup should be relatively easy to create, but for families with less access to technology, the technological requirements of online tutoring can be an accessibility barrier, and in these cases, in-person tutoring may be more effective. 

In addition to a reliable internet connection, online tutoring usually requires the use of a video platform that the tutor, student, and parent are all comfortable using. Zoom and Skype are two possible examples. The final essential part of the online tutoring setup is a quiet space for the student to work and focus. A noisy or crowded space can make it much harder for students and tutors to hear each other and can make the tutoring session more challenging for all involved. Parents and other family members can support their child's online tutoring experience by helping them reduce interruptions and distractions. 

How Do I Find an Online Tutor?

Finding the right online tutor can be an overwhelming prospect: there are so many online tutoring services and so many different educational approaches! The good news is that while this variety can seem like a barrier to starting the tutoring process, it actually means that there is something for everyone. Not all tutors have the same approach to working with students, so it is important to look at different options to see what aligns best with your needs. For instance, finding a tutor for kids online is a different process from finding a college prep tutor who specializes in a handful of national exams. 

When finding your online tutor, it is best to take several things into account. First, make sure you know exactly what you want your tutor to help with. Do you need help with trigonometry? Are you looking for someone who can help with learning a second language? Maybe you are hoping to work with a tutor who has experience with students with ADHD or other academic accessibility needs. Make sure to investigate the pedagogical approach and services offered for any tutoring platform you find. Ensure that your tutoring service operates in your time zone and has availability that suits your schedule. Essentially, the best way to approach finding an online tutor is with patience and thorough research. 

What Makes a Good Online Tutor?

Whether you are looking for online tutoring for elementary students or for high school and beyond, there are certain traits that make a particularly good tutor and certain things that you can do as a student or a parent to make sure that the tutoring relationship runs as smoothly and effectively as possible. Not all tutors will have the same approach, experience level, or subject matter expertise. This is not necessarily a fault with the tutor: some students and tutors will click with one another, and others will find that their teaching and learning styles are not aligned. Knowing the traits of a good online tutor and knowing how to start and maintain an effective connection with a tutor is one of the most important steps when starting the online tutoring process. 

Traits of a Good Online Tutor

You might decide that you need to work with a live online math tutor twice a week and find an organization that looks like a good fit, but it is still important to know what to look for in a tutor. There are many different traits that tutors can embody that can help them support their students. Not all tutors will exhibit all of the following traits, and that is not necessarily a bad thing, but some traits that are generally good for tutors to cultivate include the following: 

 Trait | Importance in Tutoring
-------|----------------------
 Adaptability | A good tutor knows that students learn in a variety of different ways. They will try to be flexible about how they deliver information in order to make their work as accessible to their students as possible. It can take time for students and tutors to get to know one another's styles, but the ability to adapt to various and changing circumstances is one that can only benefit everyone involved in the tutoring relationship.
 Patience | As with all educators, tutors need to be patient with their students. This is especially true for tutors who are helping struggling students catch up with their peers. A good tutor will not get frustrated or impatient and will be willing to go over material as many times and in as many different ways as needed for their pupils to feel comfortable with their understanding and proficiency.
 Good listening skills | Tutoring is as much about speaking to students as it is about listening to them. One of the biggest benefits of tutoring is that students can get the one-on-one attention that they possibly could be missing out on in a larger classroom. A good tutor will pay close attention to students' needs, questions, and concerns and incorporate that information into their lesson plans to make them as effective as possible.
 Clear communication skills | No matter how much of an expert a tutor is in a given field, much of their ability to be a good tutor will revolve around how they communicate information and expectations to students. Tutors should not only deliver academic information in a straightforward way, but also communicate clearly about any homework or assignments they are setting for their students in between sessions as well as any other logistical considerations that may arise.
 Approachability | Working one-on-one with a tutor can sometimes be daunting, especially through the admittedly awkward medium of video chat. A good tutor can help overcome the limitations of technology by being approachable and friendly toward students. It is important for the recipients of a tutor's services to feel comfortable in their tutoring sessions and to feel that they can ask questions and raise concerns as needed.
 Expertise | Most tutors generally have a good sense of which subjects and difficulty levels they are most comfortable teaching to students. It is important for tutors to be well-prepared and confident in their approach to the academic material that they are working with. Tutors who work for online tutoring agencies usually have the agency match them with students whose academic needs match up with their experience and areas of interest.
 Organization | As with any job, tutors need to be organized in order to be as helpful as possible for their students. This often means that they need to have access to homework or school materials in advance so that they can prepare lesson plans and activities for their sessions and make sure that everyone gets as much out of the time as possible.
 Reliability | Online tutoring schedules are usually created in advance to fit the availability of both the student and the tutor. These schedules may be subject to change if any of the parties involved has a conflict, but a good tutor should be reliably available for as many of the scheduled sessions as possible, barring emergencies.

Some students find that some of these traits are more important than others and want to prioritize tutors who exhibit the traits that they feel will be the most helpful to them. 

What to Ask Your Prospective Tutor

You have found a tutoring site that you like, and you think you know which tutor will work best for you. As a parent or student, what are the most important questions you should ask your prospective tutor to see if they will be a good fit? To begin with, make sure they have the knowledge base to help with the subjects you are interested in exploring. Asking a tutor who specializes in algebra to help with Spanish homework is unlikely to yield the best results. 

You'll want to ask the following questions: 
- What teaching experience do they have? 
- How long have they been a tutor? 
- What attracted them to the work? 
- Do they have any other related educational experience? 
- What is their personal pedagogical approach and what kinds of techniques do they favor? 
- What activities have they found effective when tutoring in the past? 
- What is their short-term and long-term availability? 

During this initial meeting, your tutor will likely also have questions for you about your goals, what has worked well and poorly in the past, and what you are hoping to get out of your tutoring experience. 

Communicating with Your Tutor

The single most important thing that parents and students can do to ensure that tutoring goes well is to always prioritize clear, effective, and regular communication with your tutor. 

You want to: 
- Be clear about your needs and expectations. 
- Explain the timeline for the kind of tutoring you want. 
- Discuss previous tutoring experience, if any. 
- Be open about what you are aiming for. 
- Be aware that while tutors can make a big difference in students' education, they cannot work magic and are not a replacement for in-class education. 

The other very important thing that you can do to communicate with your tutor is to provide any educational materials that they need to work with well in advance. Unless a tutor is working with a student daily or is taking a more hands-off role as a homework supervisor, they will need time to prepare lessons in advance. Make sure to have a plan for regular check-ins about your child's progress and future tutoring needs so that nobody is taken by surprise. 

How Much Does a Private Tutor Cost?

There is no way around it: working with a tutor costs money, and the added expense can be something that concerns families who are looking into tutoring options for their children. However, there is a very big range when it comes to tutoring costs, so regardless of your budget, you will likely be able to find a system that works for your family. Some tutoring agencies bill by the hour and others bill monthly. How much you end up paying depends on how many sessions you want to schedule and what kind of tutoring you are looking for. Help with a high school or college course like statistics could be less expensive than specialized tutoring for a particular exam. 

Tutors and tutoring agencies may charge anything from $10 to $100 per hour. Monthly tutoring packages often start at around $60 but could be several hundred dollars depending on tutors' experience levels and the number of hours of labor involved. 

More expensive is not necessarily better in this case, so it is important to do your research to see what different tutoring agencies are offering. It is worth noting that online tutors tend to have lower rates than in-person tutors since they do not have to factor in travel costs. 

Does Online Tutoring Really Work?

Online tutoring is becoming increasingly popular, but is it really an effective learning tool? As with all things, online tutoring does not work for everyone. It has its downsides, including the difficulty of communicating through technology and the potential limitations when it comes to teaching more hands-on skills. 

That said, online tutoring is effective and enjoyable for many students. It provides flexibility and personalized attention that can make all the difference when it comes to academics. Many tutors also incorporate technology into their lessons in innovative and fun ways to improve their students' engagement. Online tutoring is not a perfect system, but it has the potential to be transformative and extremely helpful for many students.

The single most important thing that parents and students can do to ensure that tutoring goes well is to always prioritize clear, effective, and regular communication with your tutor.
Tutors and tutoring agencies may charge anything from $10 to $100 per hour.
Online tutoring is not a perfect system, but it has the potential to be transformative and extremely helpful for many students.
